title: :1-13 The transfiguration part 2
scripture_text: “Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.” After six days Jesus took with him Peter, James and John the brother of James, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There, he was transfigured before them. His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. Just then there appeared before them Moses and Elijah, talking with Jesus, Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here. If you wish, I will put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.”
devotional:
While he was still speaking, a bright cloud covered them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!’’

When the disciples heard this, they fell face down to the ground, terrified. But Jesus came and touched them. “Get up,”he said. “Don’t be afraid.” When they looked up, they saw no one except Jesus. As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus instructed them, “Don’t tell anyone what you have seen, until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.’’ The disciples asked him, “Why then do the teachers of the law say that Elijah must come first?” Jesus replied, “To be sure, Elijah comes and will restore all things. But I tell you, Elijah has already come and they did not recognize him, but have done to him everything they wished. In the same way the Son of Man is going to suffer at their hands.” Then the disciples understood that he was talking to them about John the Baptist.’’

A. These men needed to experience the glory of intimacy if they were going to have a proper understanding of Jesus.

One of the problems we have is that we do not really have a category were Jesus neatly fits. We are always trying to domesticate Jesus, trying to deconstruct him, so he will fit better into our lives. Sometimes we want what I would call a ‘squeezed theology’, we want to make Him fit into the compartments we have constructed in our minds, keep Him contained and controlled. Then we see His glory, and our paper thin theology is shredded, and we discover that the Jesus we think we know, is very different to the Jesus we need to know. And the Jesus we need to know is very different to the Jesus we want to know.

Matthew tells us that Jesus took and led Peter, James and John up a high mountain. The word he uses for took "paralambanō" means; to receive near, i.e. to associate with oneself in any familiar or intimate act. I think this tells us a lot about what is going on in the heart of Jesus; He knew that the best way to correct the misunderstanding of these disciples was through intimacy and a revelation of His glory. Intimacy with Jesus heals our theology

B. These men needed to develop a theology of suffering.

One of the most dangerous things about the church in our culture, is that we have gone a long way to eradicate a theology of suffering. We have bought into the health, wealth and prosperity gospel in a very subliminal ways. We can often live with an undercurrent sense of entitlement, or deserving. We think that whole-hearted commitment to Jesus means that God owes us, and our lives should be free from suffering. We would never verbalise this kind of thinking but perhaps in our hearts we hold onto verses 1Samuel 2:30 believing that if we live right then God owes us.

We need a theology of suffering that give us the freedom to take up our cross.

I have been thinking, is it possible to follow Jesus without a theology and expectation of suffering? I wonder if our lack of appreciation of the glory of Jesus is the reason why we find it difficult to accept or cope with suffering. We need to be people who grapple to grasp the glory of Jesus so we wont be defined by our suffering and pain. When our hearts are ripped open and broken, and our lives are in bits, the hardest thing to see, but the most necessary thing to see is the glory of Jesus.

C. We need to realise that suffering and glory are often the same thing. In our world view we often compartmentalise life, we have everything boxed up in neat compartments, especially when it comes to suffering and glory. Luke tells us that Moses and Elijah were speaking to Jesus about his death Luke 9:30. They were not talking about his victory, or his splendour, or his majesty, but his death.

There are a number of verses in John 13 that clearly show the relationship between glory and suffering: -

30. ‘As soon as Judas had taken the bread, he went out. And it was night. 31 When he was gone, Jesus said, “Now the Son of Man is glorified and God is glorified in him. 32 If God is glorified in him, God will glorify the Son in himself, and will glorify him at once.’

Jesus was able to discern the hand and purpose of his father in his suffering. Jesus understood that his suffering was not a random series of undetermined events that were unrelated to the purposes and plans of God. He knew that his suffering had a built-in capacity to bring his father glory as he went through it.

D. We need to leave the past behind by learning from it. Moses and Elijah appeared and began to talk to Jesus. Moses and Elijah were pinnacles of the Jewish faith and spirituality. Moses and Elijah were representatives of the law and the prophets. Moses and Elijah, who were taught to every good Jew, teaching them how to live and please God. But now it was time to listen to a new voice. Moses and Elijah appear on the mountain before the glory of Jesus to affirm that His voice, His suffering, His Glory is the way. Moses and Elijah had fulfilled their purposes, now it was time to listen to Jesus.
prayer: Father forgive me if my heart is set on ease and comfort, and I carry a subliminal sense of entitlement.
html:
:1-13 The transfiguration part 2

Study Text: Matthew 16:28-17
“Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.” After six days Jesus took with him Peter, James and John the brother of James, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There, he was transfigured before them. His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. Just then there appeared before them Moses and Elijah, talking with Jesus, Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here. If you wish, I will put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.”

While he was still speaking, a bright cloud covered them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!’’

When the disciples heard this, they fell face down to the ground, terrified. But Jesus came and touched them. “Get up,”he said. “Don’t be afraid.” When they looked up, they saw no one except Jesus. As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus instructed them, “Don’t tell anyone what you have seen, until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.’’ The disciples asked him, “Why then do the teachers of the law say that Elijah must come first?” Jesus replied, “To be sure, Elijah comes and will restore all things. But I tell you, Elijah has already come and they did not recognize him, but have done to him everything they wished. When our hearts are ripped open and broken, and our lives are in bits, the hardest thing to see, but the most necessary thing to see is the glory of Jesus.

There are a number of verses in John 13 that clearly show the relationship between glory and suffering: -

30. ‘As soon as Judas had taken the bread, he went out. And it was night. 31 When he was gone, Jesus said, “Now the Son of Man is glorified and God is glorified in him. 32 If God is glorified in him, God will glorify the Son in himself, and will glorify him at once.’

Jesus was able to discern the hand and purpose of his father in his suffering. Jesus understood that his suffering was not a random series of undetermined events that were unrelated to the purposes and plans of God. D. We need to leave the past behind by learning from it. Moses and Elijah appeared and began to talk to Jesus. Moses and Elijah were pinnacles of the Jewish faith and spirituality. But now it was time to listen to a new voice. Moses and Elijah appear on the mountain before the glory of Jesus to affirm that His voice, His suffering, His Glory is the way. Moses and Elijah had fulfilled their purposes, now it was time to listen to Jesus.

Prayer: Father forgive me if my heart is set on ease and comfort, and I carry a subliminal sense of entitlement.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 1 Chronicles 7-10
